summary:
German Unification was the political and military process by which the disparate German-speaking states of central Europe were consolidated into a single nation-state — the German Empire — between 1864 and 1871. Orchestrated primarily by Otto von Bismarck, Minister President of Prussia, through a series of calculated wars and diplomatic maneuvers, German unification fundamentally altered the balance of power in Europe and set in motion forces that would define the twentieth century.

Before unification, the German-speaking world was organized as the loose German Confederation of 39 states established at the [[event:2523|Congress of Vienna]], dominated by the rivalry between Prussia in the north and Austria in the south. The failure of the liberal [[event:2532|Revolutions of 1848]] to unify Germany through parliamentary means convinced [[event:2574|Bismarck]] that unification could only come 'through blood and iron' — through Prussian military power rather than popular enthusiasm or liberal constitutionalism.

Bismarck's strategy proceeded in three stages. The war against Denmark in 1864 over the Schleswig-Holstein question was fought in partnership with Austria, giving Prussia control over key northern territories. The [[event:2575|Austro-Prussian War]] of 1866, sometimes called the Seven Weeks' War, decisively defeated Austria and expelled it from German affairs, dissolving the old German Confederation and replacing it with a Prussian-dominated North German Confederation. This victory was achieved so rapidly and completely that it shocked Europe and established Prussian military supremacy.

The third and decisive step was the [[event:2535|Franco-Prussian War]] of 1870–71. Bismarck maneuvered France into declaring war — most controversially through the Ems Dispatch, a doctored telegram designed to inflame French national honor — and the ensuing conflict united the south German states with the north in a common war against France. The French defeat was swift and comprehensive: Emperor Napoleon III was captured at Sedan, Paris was besieged, and France was forced to cede Alsace-Lorraine and pay a massive indemnity.

On 18 January 1871, while Paris was still under siege, the German princes gathered in the Hall of Mirrors at Versailles proclaimed the [[event:3495|German Empire]] with the Prussian king William I as Kaiser. The location was deliberately chosen for its symbolic humiliation of France. The new empire united 25 German states under Prussian leadership in a federal constitutional structure, though real power remained concentrated in Prussia and in Bismarck as imperial chancellor.

The consequences of German unification were enormous. The new German Empire was the most populous and economically dynamic state in continental Europe, and its rapid industrialization during the [[event:2583|Second Industrial Revolution]] made it a potential challenger to British commercial and industrial dominance. The annexation of Alsace-Lorraine created a permanent grievance with France that would poison European politics until the First World War. Bismarck spent the next two decades of his chancellorship constructing an elaborate alliance system designed to manage and contain the consequences of the power he had created.
